items.title: One Balls
items.summary: Uniball 207. What? That's the pen I'm using now. Technically the…
items.content_html:
Uniball 207.

What?

That's the pen I'm using now. Technically the 207 plus+ line.

Okay...

Because it uses nano fiber ink.

Pigment ink.

...

Which means it's impossible to "wash."

It bonds itself to the paper.

I'm sure other pens can do this, but I saw the dude who inspired Catch Me If You Can promote it at a bank speech.

It seems like an (inactive) attorney should want something secure in his writing arsenal.

And so I do.

It's a nice pen, if you're into that sort of thing.

And I am.

Hello?

🏏

items.title: Golden Locks
items.summary: I'm growing my hair out. This is something I haven't done…
items.content_html:
I'm growing my hair out.

This is something I haven't done since my involuntary bowl cut days.

But we way beyond bowling length here.

My sheet's curling at the bottom.

I got locks.

For the first time in my life, when I wiggle my head, my hair follows. Instead of just wiggling as I wiggle, it takes a minute to catch up.

This is part of the new me.

Or rather, today me.

But I'm not sure how long this goes.

The hair or the duration.

Grandmasters seem to have long flowing hair, though.

Or are bald.

No middle ground.

So I'll probably go one way or the other.

Give me time.

I will say, it's all pretty much silky black.

I would have expected more silver and grey by this age.

Not a complaint, an observation.

Another:

Here sits an old(ish) man.

Typing away at a musings column.

Going on and on about his silly hair.

What a blessing.

To be just right.

items.title: Can't Make You Drink
items.summary: This is a happy place. My happy place. I got a…
items.content_html:
This is a happy place.

My happy place.

I got a lot of them.

Home.

The open road.

But I repeat myself.

Maybe sometimes I rant too much.

When I should be happier.

I should be happier here.

A musings column, this is, not a tabloid.

I should be more amused.

More amusing.

And so I shall be.

Did you know there is a highway that stretches from Alaska all the way down to Argentina?

There's a 60 mile stretch of impassible rain forest smack dab in the middle but that gets in the way of a good story.

You can drive the whole thing (almost).

Pretty amazing.

Now would you want to?

I mean, that's the whole shebang, ain't it?

Horse, this is water.

Neigh!

🚰 🐎

items.title: No Sell
items.summary: I write about pro wrestling a lot more than I ever…
items.content_html:
I write about pro wrestling a lot more than I ever thought I would here.

It's not because I watch it.

The last time I did was years ago.

But I grew up with it.

It was a childhood love as deep as baseball; a young adult love as deep as weight lifting. 

Two more things I also do not do anymore.

Which is crazy to say, let alone live.

But there's one thing I have to share about pro wrestling that can help you today.

It's the art of selling.

No, no, not like that.

Selling is the insider term for going along with the action. Feeding into it. Like improv actors flow with "Yes, and..."

Meaning, someone swings at your face, and you stiffen and fall to the ground.

The falling to the ground act is "selling" the strike.

And that's a good thing in the ring, but horrible in a lot of real life situations.

All these rage baiters, spending all their time trying to get you angry.

And you scrolling or strolling, searching for something to be angry about.

You ain't Ric Flair.

Whooooooooooo!

Stop selling this sheet.

Who cares if they're saying something you do not agree with.

Did it ever occur to you that they WANT you to sell it?

So I say, be like Goldberg (first time I ever said that).

Don't sell sheet.

Shrug it off.

Roll your neck and traps.

And spear 'em.

Actually don't.

Just move on.

Doesn't move the needle.

Chill.

Be cool, baby.

No sell.

No buy.

It's terrible in pro wrestling, but vital in life.

No mas sell.

You buying this sheet?

It's on sale.

items.title: Macho Cheese
items.summary: Ooooohh yeah, it's the Macho Man. Is it too soon to…
items.content_html:
Ooooohh yeah, it's the Macho Man.

Is it too soon to claim Mach's moniker?

Dropping elbows and beef jerkyesque sticks on fools.

All while wearing sequin.

And speaking impossibly raspy.

And being kind of mean.

Especially to the people he loved.

It shouldn't have worked.

But it did.

Magnificently.

I'm a Ricky "The Dragon" Steamboat guy.

But Mach can do no wrong in this house.

Read the boots.

First name: Macho.

Last name: Man.

items.title: Do You Know Something I Don't?
items.summary: There's never been a better time to put your money where…
items.content_html:
There's never been a better time to put your money where your mouth is.

Why has gambling become so ubiquitous (<-- I had to spellcheck that)?

When I was growing up, it was frowned upon to even talk about it during sporting events.

Now, it's their main sponsors.

Commercials full of gambling sites.

Everywhere.

And today, with prediction markets added to sports gambling, you can bet on everyday life occurrences.

To occur or not to occur.

Every single moment.

You all should know by now, I'm an asymmetric bettor.

But I never really gamble.

I mean, I did.

But no longer.

(Sorta).

What I'm trying to say is, gambling is today's drug of choice.

It always has been.

But it's ok now more than ever to be an addict.

I'm sure there are other temptations, but this one is about as mainstream as booze.

The thing about prediction markets is that there appears to be little safeguards against insiders wagering on their insider information.

Most know it one way, they know it another.

And they make a killing.

By definition, asymmetry.

It sure smells not legal, but we'll leave that there.

I'm torn here.

Because I'm pro-asymmetric.

Big fan.

And nothing says small chance at a big reward quite like an online casino.

It's just asymmetric bets like I like are capped.

Your risk is limited.

And for some, the problem is the capping part.

Knowing when to stop your losses.

Taking the L and calling it a day.

If this hits, I'm even.

That's the part that worries me.

The new drug of choice is dopamine.

We produce it naturally but it can be prompted quite unnaturally.

Sitting in front of screens, large and small.

Sweaty and exhausted from a game you only spectated.

I would never tell you how to spend your hard-earned energy and time units.

But this is not the best asymmetric bet.

Especially when everyone else is doing it.

items.title: More Than One Bruce
items.summary: Boy, I think I'm running out of things to say. When…
items.content_html:
Boy, I think I'm running out of things to say.

When I was a kid, I'd stay up at night and listen to the radio.

Now, a lot of the times it was to listen to music.

But then I found a dude who would spend all night giving advice to people.

On all sorts of things from money, business, to general life advice.

His name was Bruce Williams.

He had this deep, resonant, voice and it had a little New York City tint to it, so I was hooked.

Bruce was honest, helpful, and rarely sugar coated anything.

And during a time I was searching for answers (this happens from time to time, you see), I found a mentor on the airwaves.

All of that to say Bruce has passed on.

His last show was in 2013 (by then a podcast); he retired at the young age of 81.

Passed away a few years after.

I had long ago stopped listening, I am learning all of this in retrospect.

For some beautiful people have archived his stuff online.

And I just recently found him again on YouTube.

So I've been sitting here in my little time machine.

Being a kid again.

Listening and learning.

From the wisdom of a master.

Raising a glass of Coke Zero (on ice) to late, great, BW.

"Dumfries, Virginia, hello there!"

There's only one Bruce Williams.

And I miss ya, good sir.

items.title: Energy and Time
items.summary: I used to think time was the only true currency. And…
items.content_html:
I used to think time was the only true currency.

And how you spent it, more than any other, was the master key to a good life.

I even heard this great line about how time is the only currency we spend without knowing its remaining balance.

Constantly spending from an account with an unknown, but ever diminishing, value.

But time has a partner.

Energy.

You could have all the time in the world (figuratively speaking) but if you have no juice, no oomph, it's probably going to be wasted.

And wasting time should be a felony (which it kind of is with the amount of time served to it).

As an old(ish) man, I've learned just how precious these two currencies are.

The seconds.

And the stamina.

All the time in the world with no giddyup is a sad fate.

And all the horsepower in a bad engine might be a sadder one.

But this is a happy musings column.

So let's work on this.

Where are you on the energy and time (EAT) scale?

Lots of time, no energy? 

Lots of energy, no time?

No to both?

If you have lots of both, I probably want to know how you do it.

You're likely lying or insane, but you're online, so I repeat myself.

Like everything, the EAT scale has a goldilocks zone.

Enough time. 

Enough energy.

I will tell you though.

There is infinite energy.

There is.

Infinite time?

I think so.

Just not in the way we know it.

I have tapped into infinite energy.

I have spent an eternity in a second.

And when you do it too, we feast.

Do you know the equation of power?

Not status or that garbage, I mean kinetic boom power.

Power = Energy over Time.

BOOM!

items.title: A Mused
items.summary: I want to tell you how nice it is to be…
items.content_html:
I want to tell you how nice it is to be able to type.

How much I love to write.

And I do.

I do love to write.

But there are more important things in the world.

And lately, I've been feeling called to those and them.

The more important things.

A musings column is such a selfish endeavor.

And perhaps I've spent enough time over the years doing those.

items.title: Half Penny
items.summary: What would your childhood version of you think of you now?…
items.content_html:
What would your childhood version of you think of you now?

Like 11 year old you.

What would they think?

At 11, I would think I'd now be a retired hall of famer who spent his entire career at 3B with the New York Mets.

Won a few MVPs, a few triple crowns, and, most importantly, a few championship rings.

Of course, I didn't peak in my smashing bombs days until my late 30s.

A bad time to peak.

And sadly, my fields were slow-pitch church leagues by then.

But I'd like to think 11 me would be staggered, just the same, by what's happened.

I mean, he was kinda right.

I'm sorta retired.

Sort of a hall of famer (if only in my own mind).

Won some MVF awards.

Always wear a crown or three.

And I am blessed to wear one ring.

"That's not what I meant," he'd say.

And he'd be right, of course.

It's just so much better, kid.

To dreams asked for.

And dreams given.
